---
title: "Day 1 (Monday): Foundations and Regularization"
filters:
  - shinylive
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../assets/css/slides-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
    slide-level: 2
---

## Learning objectives

- Set up a predictive workflow for supervised learning
- Diagnose underfitting and overfitting
- Understand linear vs logistic regression roles
- Introduce ridge and lasso as regularization tools

## Navigation

- [Teaching Hub](../index.qmd)
- [Curriculum Outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd)
- [Module Library](../modules/index.qmd)
- [App Library](../apps/index.qmd)
- [Next: Tuesday deck](day-02-tuesday.qmd)

## Why start simple?

- Simple models give transparent baselines
- Baselines expose feature quality and data leakage risk
- You need calibration before model complexity

## Linear regression as baseline

- Use as first supervised model to discuss fit quality
- Frame the bias-variance tradeoff
- Compare train vs validation error as complexity grows

## Linear model: matrix form

- Response vector \(y \in \mathbb{R}^n\); design matrix \(X\) with rows as samples and columns as predictors (plus intercept column).
- Ordinary least squares minimizes \(\|y - X\beta\|_2^2\) over \(\beta\).
- Closed form (when \(X'X\) is invertible): \(\hat\beta = (X'X)^{-1} X'y\).
- Predictions on new \(X_{\text{new}}\): \(\hat y_{\text{new}} = X_{\text{new}} \hat\beta\).

## What OLS gives you (and what it assumes)

- **Interpretation:** each \(\hat\beta_j\) is a *conditional* slope: expected change in \(y\) when gene \(j\) increases by one unit, holding other modeled genes fixed.
- **Linearity:** effects combine additively in the predictor space you specify (interactions only if you engineer them).
- **Collinearity:** if predictors are nearly redundant, variances of \(\hat\beta_j\) explode and coefficients become unstable—regularization is one response.
- **High \(p\), low \(n\):** OLS is not unique or behaves badly; shrinkage or explicit sparsity becomes necessary.

## Discrete complexity control: AIC stepwise search

- **AIC** trades log-likelihood against model size: \(\mathrm{AIC} = 2k - 2\log L\). Lower is better; compare models on the *same* data with care.
- **Backward** starts at the full linear model and drops terms if AIC improves.
- **Forward** starts at the intercept-only model and adds terms greedily up to a declared upper scope.
- Unlike ridge/lasso, this is a **discrete** search over subsets of predictors; it can miss good subsets and is more aggressive about *which* \(k\) counts as complexity.

## Logistic regression for classification

- Introduce probability modeling via log-odds
- Connect decision threshold to false positive/false negative tradeoff
- Discuss model assumptions and where they break

## Overfitting vs underfitting

- Underfitting: both train and validation errors high
- Overfitting: train error low, validation error high
- Good fit: balanced complexity and stable generalization

## Practical diagnostics

- Train/validation/test split as default
- Cross-validation for robust performance estimates
- Learning curves to detect data-limited regimes

## Regularization motivation

- Correlated predictors destabilize coefficient estimates
- High-dimensional data increases variance
- Regularization trades a bit of bias for lower variance

## Ridge and lasso

- Ridge: shrinks all coefficients, rarely to exact zero
- Lasso: can set coefficients to zero (feature selection)
- Choice depends on sparsity assumptions and interpretability needs

## Course examples: visuals (LASSO / ridge + logistic)

The next slides pull in live plots from the same simulations as your original `.Rmd` notebooks in `Examples/Day 1 - ...`.

{{< include ../_includes/day01-lasso-ridge-logistic.qmd >}}

## Demo: regularization paths (Shinylive)

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
# Placeholder app. Replace with full implementation in apps/regularization_path_demo/app.R
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  h4("Regularization Path Demo"),
  p("TODO: add ridge/lasso path exploration UI.")
)
server <- function(input, output, session) {}
shinyApp(ui, server)
```

## End-of-day takeaway

- Regression models are a conceptual launchpad for classification
- Regularization is essential before moving to more flexible algorithms
- Exercises are handled separately
